title: Sage and onion stuffing
subtitle: The classic British stuffing, baked in a dish
excerpt: Softened onions, fresh breadcrumbs, and dried sage bound with egg and butter, baked until the top is golden and crisp. The indispensable stuffing for roast chicken, turkey, and pork.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Sage and onion stuffing for goose and duck.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845). Forcemeat and stuffings.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before baking. Defrost in the fridge overnight and bake from room temperature.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Use a larger baking dish.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make and refrigerate the stuffing mixture up to 24 hours ahead. Bake from the fridge with an extra 10 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cooking onions gently in butter over low heat until they soften and become translucent, without browning. Essential for removing the harsh raw onion flavour from stuffings.

content.text: A properly made stuffing is baked in a dish, not inside the bird. Inside the bird, the stuffing insulates the cavity and slows the cooking of the innermost meat; in a dish, it gets a crisped top and an even cook throughout. This recipe is correct for chicken, turkey, and pork.

items.prepNote: fresh, leaves chopped (or an extra tsp dried)

content.text: Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Melt the butter in a medium saucepan over a low heat. Add the onions and
gently for 12 to 15 minutes until completely soft and translucent. Do not allow them to colour. Remove from the heat and cool slightly.

Combine the breadcrumbs, dried and fresh sage (if using), and salt in a bowl. Add the softened onions and all the butter from the pan. Mix well. Add the beaten eggs and stir until the mixture holds together.
Press into a buttered baking dish or small roasting tin. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the top is golden and the stuffing has set. The edges should be slightly crisped.

items.symptom: The stuffing is wet and dense after baking
items.cause: Too much egg, or the breadcrumbs were too fresh and moist.
items.fix: Use bread that is a day old rather than freshly made. The mixture should hold its shape when pressed but not be sticky or wet.

items.symptom: The stuffing is crumbly and falls apart when served
items.fix: The mixture needs enough beaten egg to bind the breadcrumbs. Test by pressing a handful: it should hold a shape.

items.symptom: The sage flavour is too strong
items.cause: Too much dried sage or the sage was very old and concentrated.
items.fix: Dried sage intensifies significantly during baking. Use fresh sage as the main flavour, with dried as a support.

items.symptom: The top is brown but the inside is still wet
items.cause: The dish was too deep for the baking time.
items.fix: Use a wider, shallower dish. The stuffing should be no deeper than 4 cm for even cooking.

content.text: Add a finely chopped apple to the onion mixture for a pork stuffing. For sausage meat stuffing, see the chestnut and sausagemeat stuffing recipe. A handful of chopped walnuts adds texture.

Sage and onion stuffing is the oldest surviving British stuffing recipe in common use. Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton describes it for roast goose and duck; it later transferred to chicken and turkey as those birds became the dominant British roasts. Paxo, launched in the 1950s as a dried instant version, has helped it remain in the British consciousness even as scratch cooking declined.
